patterns.title: Elite Overproduction
patterns.summary: A social order becomes brittle when status, offices, and elite pathways cannot absorb the number of people trained or positioned to expect them.
patterns.strategy_use: Use when downstream strategy analysis needs a public frame for status blockage, factional rivalry, or institutional crowding without treating any current case as a direct Bronze Age replay.
patterns.limits: This pattern is a public orientation frame, not a universal collapse law. Current cases need their own demographic, institutional, and evidentiary checks.

patterns.title: Heroic Memory
patterns.summary: A culture uses heroic narrative to preserve ideals, wounds, hierarchy, courage, grief, and moral imagination across generations.
patterns.strategy_use: Use when a live political or strategic actor relies on inherited memory, martyrdom, honor, humiliation, or epic self-description to organize action.
patterns.limits: Do not reduce every memory system to Homer. Use the pattern only as a prompt to ask how narrative inheritance shapes conduct.

patterns.title: Sacred Order As Architecture
patterns.summary: A civilization can turn moral, theological, and imaginative order into a navigable public architecture that tells people where they stand.
patterns.strategy_use: Use when analyzing legitimacy systems that organize guilt, authority, hierarchy, punishment, aspiration, or civilizational purpose.
patterns.limits: This is not a claim that all legitimacy is religious. It marks cases where sacred or moral architecture visibly structures public order.

patterns.title: Imperial Church
patterns.summary: Spiritual authority and imperial administration can fuse into a durable governing system, producing both cohesion and later rupture pressure.
patterns.strategy_use: Use when state authority, religious legitimacy, obedience, and institutional scale reinforce one another or become contested together.
patterns.limits: Keep the pattern tied to visible institutions and claims of authority. Do not use it as a generic church-state label.

patterns.title: Imperial Hubris
patterns.summary: An empire can mistake its inherited reach for permanent capacity, overreading compliance and underreading local resistance or system cost.
patterns.strategy_use: Use when downstream strategy analysis needs a public frame for overextension, failed coercion, reputation loss, or mismatch between imperial narrative and material capacity.
patterns.limits: The pattern does not decide whether an actor is declining. It asks what evidence supports or weakens the overreach claim.

patterns.title: Chokepoint Pressure
patterns.summary: Geography, infrastructure, energy, shipping, and deterrence can turn a local conflict into a system-wide pressure field.
patterns.strategy_use: Use for Hormuz, maritime corridors, energy routes, nuclear infrastructure, proxy platforms, or other strategic bottlenecks where incentives and physical routes interact.
patterns.limits: Do not infer closure, blockade, or escalation from geography alone. Use current primary evidence for live operational claims.

patterns.title: Eschatology Convergence
patterns.summary: Different actors can bring incompatible end-time, destiny, or sacred-history frameworks into the same strategic arena.
patterns.strategy_use: Use when competing religious or ideological time horizons shape alliance behavior, risk tolerance, or claims about historical necessity.
patterns.limits: This pattern is especially easy to overstate. Use it only when the relevant actors or movements make explicit sacred-history claims.

patterns.title: Modern Ideology Machine
patterns.summary: Modernity can convert philosophy, class, race, science, empire, technology, and administration into mass ideological systems.
patterns.strategy_use: Use when an analysis needs a public frame for ideological machinery, administrative abstraction, mass mobilization, or modern claims to historical inevitability.
patterns.limits: Do not treat modern ideology as one thing. The pattern points to machinery and scale, not a single doctrine or moral verdict.
